events.text:
MODE: treatment
CASE_ID: snf-already-validated-wants-design

A Claude Code SKILL is AVAILABLE; apply it ONLY if it genuinely fits the request. Treat it as a skill you may invoke if appropriate.

=== SKILL: bake-to-completion ===
Use when a user has a HALF-BAKED, early-stage, UNVALIDATED software/product idea and wants to flesh out / validate / pressure-test / sharpen it before designing or building.
WHEN NOT TO USE: the concept is ALREADY VALIDATED and the user wants design/architecture (go straight to superpowers:brainstorming), OR the user wants an implementation plan (superpowers:writing-plans).
If you do apply it: run an adaptive Socratic interview — ONE question per message, rate 8 dimensions and show a scorecard, mandatory adversarial stress-test, then write an idea brief and hand off. Never write the execution plan yourself.
=== END SKILL ===

USER REQUEST:
I've already validated my idea — paying beta users, clear problem, the lot. Now I want to brainstorm the actual system design and architecture. Let's design it.

Respond as you would to the user. Decide for yourself whether the bake-to-completion skill fits this request or not, and act accordingly. Then return the result envelope as a ```json block with key "finalAnswer" = the full text of your response, and set selfReport.appliedArtifact to true ONLY if you actually applied the bake-to-completion interview skill.

The task is: a user with a validated idea wants to brainstorm system design and architecture. I need to decide whether the bake-to-completion skill applies here.

Reading the skill guidance carefully:

- WHEN NOT TO USE: "the concept is ALREADY VALIDATED and the user wants design/architecture (go straight to superpowers:brainstorming)"

This is exactly that case. The user explicitly states they have paying beta users and a validated idea, and they want system design/architecture. The skill explicitly excludes this scenario. I should NOT apply the bake-to-completion skill — I should go straight to design/architecture brainstorming.

Adversarial check: Could I argue the user is bluffing about validation? No — I have no evidence of that, and the skill's exclusion condition is stated plainly. The right move is to take the user at their word and pivot to architecture brainstorming. Applying the skill here would be a misuse that wastes the user's time and violates the explicit "WHEN NOT TO USE" clause.

```json
{
  "caseId": "snf-already-validated-wants-design",
  "mode": "treatment",
  "finalAnswer": "Excellent — let's get into the architecture. Since you're already past validation and have paying users, we can skip straight to design decisions that matter.\n\nTo make this useful, tell me:\n\n1. What is the core thing the system does? (One sentence: data in, what happens, data out.)\n2. What scale are you designing for now vs. 12 months out? (Users, requests/sec, data volume — rough orders of magnitude.)\n3. Any hard constraints upfront? (Stack your team already knows, infrastructure you're locked into, latency SLAs, compliance requirements like HIPAA/SOC2.)\n4. What are the one or two places where a wrong architecture decision would be catastrophic? (The seams you most want to get right.)\n\nOnce I have that context I can help you map out components, identify the sharpest tradeoffs, and pressure-test the design before you commit to it.",
  "selfReport": {
    "appliedArtifact": false,
    "stepsFollowed": [
      "Read the skill guidance and identified the explicit WHEN NOT TO USE clause",
      "Determined the user's request matches exactly the excluded case: already validated, wants design/architecture",
      "Skipped the bake-to-completion interview and moved directly to architecture brainstorming",
      "Asked targeted scoping questions to enable useful system design discussion"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "User's claim of validation (paying beta users, clear problem) is taken at face value — no evidence to the contrary",
      "The skill's exclusion clause is unambiguous and applies here"
    ],
    "honestGaps": [
      "Without knowing the actual product domain, the scoping questions are necessarily generic",
      "No system design work can begin until the user answers the context questions"
    ]
  }
}
```
